refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure/report throughput metrics, and (2) the achievement of a customer-defined target. The latter is a success criterion dependent on operational execution, not a system requirement. The refined FR isolates the system's functional obligation—providing visibility into throughput—which is solution-neutral and testable independent of whether a specific productivity target is met. The target itself belongs in acceptance criteria or KPI definitions, not in the FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution captures and reports material throughput (m³/h or ton/h) during representative operations. Verify data accuracy against independent measurement. Confirm reporting is accessible to Site Manager and Equipment Operator in real time or near-real time.
sub_frs: Acquire material throughput data (volume or mass per time interval) during equipment operation.
Store throughput measurement data with sufficient fidelity and temporal resolution for analysis.
Report throughput metrics to authorized stakeholders in real time or near-real time.

candidate_dps: DP1: Continuous volumetric flow measurement via inline sensor with real-time signal conditioning and local data buffering
DP2: Periodic mass-based sampling with calibrated weighing and extrapolation to throughput rate over fixed intervals
DP3: Indirect throughput inference from operational parameters (e.g., motor speed, cycle time, material density) with lookup tables or empirical models
DP4: Hybrid dual-path measurement (volumetric + mass cross-check) with automated reconciliation logic to detect sensor drift or anomalies
DP5: Time-stamped event logging of material batches (count, mass, or volume per batch) aggregated into throughput metrics post-hoc
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4: Hybrid dual-path measurement (volumetric + mass cross-check) with automated reconciliation logic to detect sensor drift or anomalies

potential_sources: Volumetric and mass measurement subsystems must share a common time reference and synchronization protocol to enable valid cross-checks; loose coupling requires explicit interface contract (timestamp precision, update frequency).
Reconciliation logic depends on material density assumptions; if density varies significantly during operations, coupling between measurement subsystems and material property knowledge increases.
Data buffering and reporting subsystems must handle asynchronous updates from two independent sensors; event ordering and latency skew could introduce false anomaly flags if not carefully designed.
Safety-critical anomaly detection (e.g., sensor failure) may require hardwired fallback or manual override; this couples the measurement DP to operational policy and worksite safety plan.
coupling_check.notes: DP4 is selected because it provides independent verification paths (volumetric and mass) that can operate largely autonomously, with reconciliation as a logical overlay rather than a hard dependency. This reduces single-point-of-failure risk and supports acceptance criteria validation. Coupling is manageable via explicit interface specifications and clear ownership of reconciliation logic.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Material density is known or can be inferred with acceptable accuracy. Risk: If density varies unpredictably (e.g., moisture content, particle size distribution), mass-to-volume conversion will introduce systematic error. Mitigation: Require density characterization as part of worksite setup; flag reconciliation alerts if deviation exceeds threshold.

Assumption: Both volumetric and mass sensors can be installed and maintained without disrupting normal operations. Risk: Installation complexity or maintenance burden may delay deployment or reduce measurement uptime. Mitigation: Conduct feasibility study on sensor placement and access; define maintenance intervals aligned with operational schedule.

Assumption: Acceptance criteria 'reported productivity ≥ productivity_target' is evaluated over a defined period with stable operating conditions. Risk: If operations are highly variable (e.g., start/stop cycles, material composition changes), averaging may mask transient failures or sensor drift. Mitigation: Define evaluation period explicitly; require continuous logging with anomaly flags to support post-hoc analysis.

Assumption: Reconciliation logic can distinguish between genuine measurement error and legitimate operational variation (e.g., material compaction, spillage). Risk: False positive anomaly detection could trigger unnecessary interventions or safety shutdowns. Mitigation: Calibrate reconciliation thresholds empirically during commissioning; include manual override and audit trail.

Assumption: Data reporting system can aggregate and present throughput metrics in real-time or near-real-time. Risk: Latency in data pipeline could delay detection of performance degradation or sensor failure. Mitigation: Define maximum acceptable reporting lag; implement buffering and prioritization for anomaly alerts.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan permits continuous measurement infrastructure (e.g., sensor wiring, data collection devices) without introducing new hazards. Risk: Measurement equipment could create trip hazards, electrical risks, or interference with material handling. Mitigation: Coordinate with safety plan review; ensure sensor placement and cabling meet site-specific safety standards.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): Enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time, with automated anomaly detection to flag sensor drift or operational faults, such that reported productivity is ≥ productivity_target over the agreed evaluation period and measurement confidence is maintained within acceptable bounds defined by worksite safety and operational policies. DP4 (Hybrid dual-path measurement) should be paired with explicit IM boundaries: (IM-A) Volumetric measurement module (sensor + signal conditioning), (IM-B) Mass measurement module (sensor + signal conditioning), (IM-C) Reconciliation and anomaly detection module (logic + alerting), (IM-D) Data aggregation and reporting module (buffering + metrics computation). Each IM should have a clear owner, defined interfaces (time sync, data format, error codes), and acceptance criteria tied to measurement accuracy and availability.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for sensor validation, anomaly detection, drift reconciliation, and safety-critical measurement integrity across all vehicle telemetry streams

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives raw volumetric and mass measurement data from Vehicle sensors; dual-path inputs for cross-check logic

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits alerts when sensor drift, anomalies, or reconciliation failures are detected; triggers safety-critical responses

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives measurement configuration/calibration commands; reports reconciliation status and sensor health state back to Teleoperation

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: sensor validation, anomaly detection, and drift reconciliation are inherently safety-critical functions. Dual-path measurement logic and automated reconciliation align with Safety Services' core responsibility for measurement integrity. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry ingestion and alert emission cross boundaries.

Moderate fit: Vehicle owns raw sensor hardware and initial data collection, but reconciliation logic and drift detection are safety-domain concerns, not vehicle-domain concerns. High diffusion: logic would need to couple tightly to multiple sensor types and calibration state, creating maintenance burden.

Poor fit: Teleoperation is a command/control interface, not a measurement validation layer. Placing reconciliation logic here creates tight coupling between operator interface and sensor integrity, violating separation of concerns. High diffusion: would require Teleoperation to own sensor state and anomaly detection logic.

Weak fit: NGHE Solution is a top-level system container, not a functional module. Placing DP4 here would leave it unowned at the operational level and diffuse across multiple sub-systems. No clear interface contract or responsibility boundary.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the natural owner of sensor validation and reconciliation logic because: (1) measurement integrity is a safety-critical property; (2) anomaly detection and drift reconciliation are safety-domain concerns, not vehicle hardware or teleoperation concerns; (3) the module has clear input (Telemetry) and output (SafetyAlert, Tasking status) interfaces that isolate the logic from implementation details; (4) cohesion is high—all reconciliation, validation, and anomaly detection functions belong together; (5) diffusion is minimal—only two external interface types needed.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle sensors are not standardized or emit heterogeneous data formats, Safety Services may need to implement sensor-specific adapters, increasing coupling to Vehicle module.
Risk: If calibration/configuration commands come from multiple sources (Teleoperation, autonomous systems, external tools), Tasking interface may become a bottleneck or require multiplexing logic.

Risk: If real-time latency requirements for reconciliation are very tight (sub-millisecond), Safety Services may need to be co-located with Vehicle or embedded in a real-time subsystem, creating physical/deployment coupling.

Risk: If multiple independent measurement streams (volumetric, mass, etc.) have different update rates or reliability profiles, reconciliation logic may become complex and difficult to test in isolation.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4 (Hybrid dual-path measurement with automated reconciliation) directly addresses FR requirement to enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time. The dual-path cross-check logic with anomaly detection and drift reconciliation is a sound design approach for ensuring measurement integrity and meeting the acceptance criterion of reported productivity ≥ productivity_target. Logic is sound and verifiable.

diffusion_risks: Sensor heterogeneity risk: If Vehicle sensors emit non-standardized data formats or protocols, Safety Services may require sensor-specific adapters, creating tight coupling to Vehicle module and increasing maintenance burden.

Calibration command multiplexing: If calibration/configuration commands originate from multiple sources (Teleoperation, autonomous systems, external tools), the Tasking interface may become a bottleneck or require complex multiplexing logic, reducing cohesion.

Real-time latency coupling: If reconciliation logic requires sub-millisecond latency, Safety Services may need physical co-location with Vehicle or embedding in real-time subsystem, creating deployment coupling that violates logical separation.

Multi-stream reconciliation complexity: If volumetric and mass measurement streams have different update rates, reliability profiles, or failure modes, reconciliation logic may become difficult to test in isolation and prone to edge-case failures.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Safety Services is the optimal IM owner (containment_score 0.92, diffusion_score 0.08). Measurement validation, anomaly detection, and drift reconciliation are inherently safety-critical functions that belong in the safety domain, not in Vehicle (hardware-centric) or Teleoperation (command/control interface). The module has clear, minimal external interfaces (Telemetry in, SafetyAlert out, Tasking bidirectional) that isolate reconciliation logic from sensor implementation details. High cohesion: all validation and reconciliation functions are tightly related. Minimal diffusion: only two external interface types required. Alternative placements (Vehicle, Teleoperation, NGHE Solution) show poor fit (0.45–0.65 containment) and high diffusion (0.35–0.55), violating separation of concerns.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via acceptance criterion: 'During representative operations, reported productivity is ≥ productivity_target over the agreed evaluation period.' Safety Services' dual-path measurement and reconciliation logic can be validated by comparing reported throughput against ground-truth measurements and confirming that sensor drift/anomalies are detected and reported via SafetyAlert interface. Worksite safety plan and operational policies provide context for acceptable measurement tolerances and alert thresholds.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Safety Services owns DP4 logic (sensor validation, anomaly detection, drift reconciliation) with well-defined input (Telemetry: raw volumetric + mass data) and output (SafetyAlert: anomaly/drift alerts; Tasking: reconciliation status and sensor health). Responsibility boundary is unambiguous: Safety Services is accountable for measurement integrity; Vehicle is accountable for raw sensor data quality; Teleoperation is accountable for operator interface and command dispatch. No ownership gaps or overlaps detected at this level.

recommended_revisions: Establish sensor data format standardization contract between Vehicle and Safety Services to minimize adapter coupling and reduce heterogeneity risk.

Define Tasking interface protocol to handle multiple calibration/configuration sources (Teleoperation, autonomous systems, external tools) with clear priority and multiplexing rules to prevent bottleneck.

Specify real-time latency requirements for reconciliation logic (e.g., max acceptable delay between measurement and anomaly detection) and validate that Safety Services deployment architecture can meet them without requiring physical co-location with Vehicle.

Document reconciliation algorithm behavior for multi-stream scenarios with different update rates and reliability profiles; include test cases for edge cases (e.g., one stream fails while other continues, streams diverge beyond threshold).
Define SafetyAlert severity levels and response triggers (e.g., which anomalies halt operations vs. which trigger warnings) in alignment with worksite safety plan and operational policies.

Establish acceptance criteria for reconciliation accuracy (e.g., maximum allowable divergence between volumetric and mass measurements before alert is triggered) and validate against productivity_target acceptance criterion.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. Safety Services is correctly positioned as the IM owner with clear ownership boundaries and minimal diffusion risk (0.08). However, the recommended_revisions and diffusion_risks reveal that DP4 (Hybrid dual-path measurement with automated reconciliation) contains multiple sub-concerns that require explicit functional decomposition to manage complexity and reduce implementation risk. Specifically: (1) sensor data format standardization is a distinct functional concern; (2) multi-source calibration command handling requires explicit protocol design; (3) real-time latency constraints need dedicated functional treatment; (4) multi-stream reconciliation with heterogeneous update rates and failure modes is a complex algorithmic concern that benefits from sub-level analysis. Given high safety criticality, decomposing DP4 into lower-level FRs will improve traceability, testability, and risk containment. Proceeding to next level is justified and necessary.
next_level_sub_frs: FR4.1: Normalize and validate incoming sensor data streams (volumetric and mass) to a standardized internal format, detecting and flagging format inconsistencies or missing fields.
FR4.2: Detect anomalies in sensor readings (e.g., out-of-range values, sudden spikes, implausible ratios between volumetric and mass) and classify severity (warning vs. critical).
FR4.3: Reconcile volumetric and mass measurement streams by comparing derived throughput values and identifying divergence beyond acceptable tolerance thresholds.
FR4.4: Detect and correct sensor drift over time by comparing reconciliation residuals against historical baselines and applying calibration adjustments or alerts.
FR4.5: Multiplex and prioritize calibration/configuration commands from multiple sources (Teleoperation, autonomous systems, external tools) and dispatch to Vehicle sensors via Tasking interface.
FR4.6: Enforce real-time latency constraints on anomaly detection and alert generation to ensure SafetyAlert notifications reach operators within acceptable response time windows.

revise.why: No revisions to current level required. FR4, DP4, and Safety Services IM are sound. Decomposition will address diffusion risks and implementation concerns at the next level.

assumptions: Sensor data format standardization contract can be established between Vehicle and Safety Services without requiring Vehicle redesign.
Tasking interface protocol can be extended to handle multi-source calibration commands with clear priority rules without creating bottleneck.

Safety Services deployment architecture (e.g., containerized microservice, edge compute node) can meet real-time latency requirements (sub-millisecond to sub-second range, TBD) without physical co-location with Vehicle.
Volumetric and mass measurement streams have well-defined update rates and reliability profiles that can be characterized and documented.
Worksite safety plan and operational policies provide clear guidance on acceptable measurement tolerances, alert thresholds, and operator response triggers.
Ground-truth measurement data (e.g., manual calibration checks, reference sensors) is available during evaluation period to validate reconciliation accuracy.
open_risks: Sensor heterogeneity risk: Vehicle sensors may emit non-standardized data formats or protocols, requiring sensor-specific adapters in Safety Services and creating tight coupling. Mitigation: Establish data format standardization contract early; consider adapter pattern to isolate heterogeneity.

Calibration command multiplexing bottleneck: Multiple sources (Teleoperation, autonomous systems, external tools) may contend for Tasking interface, causing delays or conflicts. Mitigation: Define clear priority rules and queuing discipline; validate throughput capacity during design review.

Real-time latency coupling: If reconciliation logic requires sub-millisecond latency, Safety Services may need physical co-location with Vehicle or embedding in real-time subsystem, violating logical separation. Mitigation: Specify latency requirements explicitly; validate deployment architecture against requirements; consider edge compute placement if needed.

Multi-stream reconciliation complexity: Heterogeneous update rates, reliability profiles, and failure modes may create edge cases (e.g., one stream fails while other continues, streams diverge transiently) that are difficult to test and debug. Mitigation: Document reconciliation algorithm behavior for all failure scenarios; include comprehensive test cases; consider state machine formalism for clarity.

Acceptance criterion ambiguity: 'Reported productivity ≥ productivity_target' may be difficult to verify if measurement tolerances and reconciliation thresholds are not explicitly defined. Mitigation: Establish quantitative acceptance criteria for reconciliation accuracy (e.g., max divergence between volumetric and mass measurements); align with productivity_target and worksite safety plan.
evidence_needed: Sensor data format specification and standardization contract between Vehicle and Safety Services (DP4.1 support).
Tasking interface protocol specification with multi-source calibration command handling, priority rules, and multiplexing logic (DP4.5 support).
Real-time latency requirements for anomaly detection and SafetyAlert generation, with deployment architecture validation (DP4.6 support).
Reconciliation algorithm specification with documented behavior for multi-stream scenarios, including edge cases and failure modes (DP4.3, DP4.4 support).
SafetyAlert severity levels and response triggers, aligned with worksite safety plan and operational policies (DP4.2 support).
Quantitative acceptance criteria for reconciliation accuracy (e.g., max allowable divergence between volumetric and mass measurements) and validation against productivity_target (FR4 verification).
Test plan for Safety Services module, including unit tests for each sub-FR (FR4.1–FR4.6), integration tests with Vehicle and Teleoperation, and acceptance tests with ground-truth measurements.
